narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family income background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 59.8% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help ensure we admit students most likely to succeed. The biggest factor pushing toward this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which was in the bottom 10%—much lower than the typical student's mid-range position—and this stood out as the strongest signal of potential struggle on the bar. On a positive note, your LSAT score of 38, above the average of about 37, strongly pulled the prediction in the other direction, suggesting your test-taking skills are a real asset. Your family income also helped, as upper-middle status is linked to better outcomes compared to lower brackets in the dataset. Smaller influences included your Year 1 decile in the lower ranks, which nudged toward failure risk, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly below the typical 3.21, adding a bit more concern. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority who succeed, offering a slight positive boost. Overall, compared to typical students who pass, your mid-tier LSAT and income were competitive, but the low Year 3 grades dominated the decision, outweighing those positives. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome early setbacks. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, perhaps retaking undergrad classes or pursuing a post-bac program to lift your GPA. You might also seek bar prep resources early or connect with mentors from similar backgrounds who've succeeded. We're rooting for you; reapply after addressing these areas, and consider schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story as a Black woman in law. Keep pushing forward—you've got this.
